title: Learn AI-Assisted Coding with Me: An Advent of Code Challenge
author: Shane Duan
text:
Learn AI-Assisted Coding with Me: An Advent of Code Challenge
One rule: let AI write all the code.
This December, I’m doing Advent of Code with one restriction: I won’t write a single line of code myself. AI writes everything. I’m inviting you to join me.
Why I’m Thinking About This
As Director of Engineering at Rockfish Data, one of my ongoing responsibilities is figuring out how to build and grow a performing software development team. It’s been a professional passion throughout my career. With AI-assisted coding becoming real, I’ve seen a lot of leaders respond with mandates. “Use AI 50% of the time!” That kind of thing. It feels lazy to me. It doesn’t help anyone actually get better.
I want something more thought through — something that can be part of real career coaching. That means I need to understand this shift myself, not just talk about it.
Two Different Situations for Software Engineers
For senior engineers, there are two equally good paths. You can stick to what you know, become the equivalent of the mainframe programmer at the turn of the century — make a good living and ride into the sunset. Or you can adopt AI as the most powerful tool of your career and become many times more productive.
For junior engineers, it’s a catch-22. The traditional path requires laboring through many coding tasks across many projects — big and small, success and failure, easy and stressful. That’s how you build craftsmanship. But with AI-assisted coding, those tasks increasingly go to senior engineers who can finish them faster with AI help. Sometimes it’s time pressure. Sometimes it’s just easier than coaching. Either way, the old career path is disappearing.
I’ve heard this same concern from trusted friends across the software industry. And it resonates strongly with the hierarchy of software engineering that Andrew Ng laid out recently. You can watch it here from 15:37 AI Fund’s GP, Andrew Ng: LLMs as the Next Geopolitical Weapon & Do Margins Still Matter in AI?
So, what’s the new path? I think it’s this: learn clean design and abstract thinking, learn to instruct AI clearly, and learn to evaluate results critically. The craft is shifting from writing code to directing code. And this challenge is a way to practice that shift together.
Promising Initial Experiment
I tested this on last year’s Advent of Code problems with Claude Code and got extremely encouraging results.
While the problem could easily be solved in the good old way, the experience was like riding a self-driven car instead of driving yourself: Instead of focusing line by line, looking up syntax, managing the mental overhead of switching between design and implementation — I stayed at the strategic level. I thought through the abstract algorithm and directed AI to handle the details. It felt more relaxed. More like the work I actually enjoy.
My biggest “aha moment”: I didn’t need to change my workflow much. I still reviewed the code and thought about refactoring. I still cared about clean design. But instead of jumping into the details and planning every step myself, I gave clear high-level instructions, and AI handled it fast and well. I even did refactor to extract domain objects with meaningful behavior contracts. The clean design immediately proved its value in part 2 of the problem.
You can see my prompts and commits here: Issues · wolfdancer/advent.code. The issues show the sequence of prompts and the code updates from each one.
This is how you can join me this year on December 1st
- Create a public GitHub repo for your prompts and generated code.
- Each day, create one or more GitHub issues with your main prompt. Instruct your AI tool to read the prompt and produce the code.
- Keep follow-up prompts minimal. If you need many follow-ups, fix your original prompt and start over. The goal is to get better at clear instructions.
- Use separate sessions for separate tasks. Document each prompt through a github issue. Check in code associated with each prompt.
- Treat it like professional work. Think about variable naming, class design, code quality. Use as many prompts as you need to produce the code that you want to maintain. I’m skipping tests for this experiment to keep things simple. Plus, the answer to each puzzle serves as a good acceptance test.
- Show off your work and let’s compare.
The Invitation
Whether you’re a senior engineer curious about AI tools, or a junior engineer figuring out what skills to build — this is a chance to learn together. I’ll be sharing my progress throughout December. Drop a link to your repo in the comments — I’d love to follow along.
Let’s figure out what this new craft looks like.
